entry.title: Integration Guardrails are more than Integration Patterns of the Compossible Business
entry.content:
What are Integration Patterns? Are those Guardrails? Or part of the overall Framework? Or just “simple” Building Blocks, either Architecture (ABBs) or Solution (SBBs)… It can be all, depending on the depth we want to go.

But things are changing.

Now we talk about Compossible Business Architecture, again, continuing the story from the previous article[1][2][3]. We see Composable Business Architecture as a “style” build for Resilience, and more. Of course, Compossible Business Architecture is not only about Resilience, but also about Agility, Speed, and about overall Leadership. It is by far more than just “technology”, but I will stick with the technology (at least a bit) in the article.

What is that what can enable us to be resilient, agile and have speed to react in the Intelligent Enterprise? We may need to adopt, to introduce now business process, replace some ABBs, or add some new and different SBBs. What is the glue connection all this?

It’s all about integrations… Integrations are seamlessly connecting everything with everyone. After all, aren’t we talking of Boundaryless Information Flow©?[4]?

Tides are shifting?

While in the “past” we could allow ourselves to be “framework” specific, things are changing now.

In previous articles (Part 1, Part 2, Part 3 and then Part 4 covering Omni-channel example) I was already talking about Integration Patterns, but focus was mostly around SAP Integration Solution Advisory Methodology (ISA-M)[5].

With building Composable Business, we are now distancing ourselves from the specific frameworks and methodologies, or even away from the specific products. The idea is “everything“ is just a ”replicable” box, and every box can be replaced with other box(s). We are looking at the pattens – only (high level) patterns. If and how those patterns are “manageable” with specific frameworks and/or methodologies, at least as specific Solution Guardrails – well, this is a different story, for some other article (perhaps?)…  

NEW Integration Patterns – simplified approach…

We should don’t try to overwhelm ourselves too much with terminology mess – what is Sync or Async. What is PULL vs. PUSH, what is batch vs. single record, is it Event or Data Streaming, is it Data Integration or Integration Service etc.

Look, we are always integrating Data, this integration can be triggered by Event (i.e. System or user-action) or schedule job, which will either PULL or PUSH some “message” sending or requesting some payload/dataset through API/EventAPI/message (i.e. message like AMQP wire protocol, which is not API), and this payload/dataset may contain some Notification, or one full Record, or Batch of many Records (which of course can be some delta or full “table”). Simple as that…

Things are not that complex?

Actually, they are, this is why AI is still not doing my job…

We will now observe only five distinct Integration Patterns and put all in one “simple” table:

#

Integration Pattern

Data Integration Context
(=Data Integration Pattern)

Method
(=Integration Function)

Data Integration Scope

Execution Style

 #

Question?

WHY

HOW

WHAT

WHEN

1

Sync Request-Reply
(=Sync Request-Response)

One-way synchronization;
Two-way synchronization;
Correlation;

(Full CRUD) Consumer(s) PUSH to Provider:
Create request (POST),
Update request (PATCH/PUT), Delete request (DELETE)
& Provider provides Confirmation reply;
Consumer(s) PUSH to Provider:
Read request (GET) & PULL Data

Single Record (or part of it);
Partial Dataset; 
Full Dataset (exceptionally i.e. Read request for small objects)

Sync as a single thread – sending request and receiving reply within single thread. 
Invoked by event on the Consumer(s)  side (i.e. user action, scheduled job)

2

Async Request-Reply
(=Async Request-Response)

One-way synchronization;
Two-way synchronization;

Provider PUSH to Consumer(s):
Replicate request (Create/Update/Delete),
& Consumer(s) PUSH to Provider Confirmation reply;

Single Record; 
Partial Dataset (exceptionally i.e. Bulk Replicate request with reply); 
Full Dataset (very exceptionally i.e. Bulk Replicate request with reply for small objects);

Async as a multi thread – sending request and receiving reply in separate threads, working in parallel with other threads. 
Invoked by event on the Provider side (i.e. System or user action, scheduled job)

3

PubSub

Broadcast; 
Data Correlation; 
Data Aggregation;

Provider PUSH to Consumer(s):
Notification request; 

Single Record (or part of it); 
Partial Dataset – exceptionally (i.e. Bulk Replicate request); 
Full Dataset – very exceptionally (i.e. Bulk Replicate request for small objects);

Async as a multi thread – sending request in parallel with other threads. 
Invoked by event on the Provider side (i.e. System or user action, scheduled job

5

E(T)L

Data Migration; 
Data Aggregation;

Middleware PULL from the Provider and PUSH into Consumer(s)

Partial Dataset (i.e. Bulk Replicate request); 
Full Dataset –exceptionally (i.e. Bulk Replicate request for small objects);

Async as a multi thread – sending request in parallel with other threads. 
Invoked by event on the middleware (i.e. scheduled job

6

DB Data Replication

Data Aggregation

Provider CDC (Change-Data-Capture) on the table level PUSH all record changes (Create/Update/Delete) to the Consumer(s)

Single Record

Sync as a single thread – continuous synchronization invoked by CDC on the table level.

But to connect with “old” terminology, lets re-explain a bit Data Integration Context:

  • Data Migration –one-time bulk data is PUSH/PULL from the Provider to the Consumer(s). Usually there is one Consumer).
  • Broadcast – data is PUSH from the Provider to the Consumer(s). Usually this is an Event based.
  • One-way synchronization – data is PUSH (or PULL in case of Sync Request-Reply GET call) from the Consumer to the Provider(s); where Consumer or the initiating System is notified of the synchronization status, either in Sync or Async mode.
  • Two-way synchronization – although one System is “formally” an owner (or Provider) of the Data Object, both (or many) Systems can initiate synchronization; data is PUSH (or PULL in case of Sync Request-Reply GET call) from one System to the other System(s); where each initiating System is notified of the synchronization status, either in Sync or Async mode. I.e. maintaining and synchronizing Vendor/Supplier between SAP MDG and SAP Ariba.
  • Data Correlation – both (or many) Systems are natural & independent Providers of the Data Object; intersection of data which is created naturally & independently is PUSH (or PULL in case of Sync Request-Reply GET call) from one System to the other System(s); where each initiating System is notified of the synchronization status. I.e. two Sales Agents creates the same Account in the different Systems – those Accounts will be synchronized, but if only one Sales Agent creates Account in one System – this Account will not be synchronized to the other System (will not be visible to the other Sales Agent);
  • Data Aggregation – there can be many Provider(s) but only one Consumer of the Data Object; data is PUSH (or PULL in case of Sync Request-Reply GET call) from the Provider(s) to the Consumer. i.e. pulling off the IoT readings; or ETL into DWH.

It is also wort mentioning some special Integration Scenarios:

  • Master Data Replication – by nature this is done in Async mode, but in majority of the cases we need to know the synchronizations status of the overall Data Object, in in some cases this is one single flow process. I.e. replication of the Business Partner Customer, related Business Partner Contact Persons, and its “link” via Business Partner Relationships.
  • EDI – this B2B Integration Scenario is specific Async integration style. I.e. for some EDI/AS2 messages Trade Partner will provide reply, but in some cases, this may not be mandatory nor expected.

What is visible here, none of the “old” or “special” patterns/scenarios in fact is clearly defining what exact Integration Patterns to apply. So, it can be misleading and prone to different interpretations. Here “new” Integration Patterns (and there are only five of them) is very specific and clear to understand – and of course, to implement.

First, it starts with Integration Principles…

Integration Principles are an equivalent to Architecture Principles – expressing our statement, and foundation rules we observe while designing the solutions.

There could be many, and it depends on form organization to organization. I.e. some examples could be

  1. Reuse existing Integration Services whenever possible – this implies we build for reusability, we do not create “multiple” instances for the same Integration Service for different Consumers. We try to build Integration Service once on the Provider and then reused by many Consumers.
  2. Consumer driven integration not only API driven – while this may sound a bit controversial, with this concept we will fully enforce layered API approach: System API, Business API and Experience API.
    Provider(s) provide System APIs/EventAPIs, but those might not meet holistic needs for cross-platform “reuse”.
    Therefore, gateways and middleware will provide Business APIs/EventAPIs, to meet most of the cross-platform “reuse”.
    Finally, individual Consumers may in fact still need to build on top it (i.e. combining several BusinessAPIs/EventAPIs), its own Experience APIs/EventAPIs, to meet the specific “customer experience” needs.
  3. Governance always – build something we can understand (i.e. Documentation), we can measure (i.e. Testing and Performance Testing) and we can manage (i.e. Monitoring and Observability implemented).
  4. Decoupled/Lousily-coupled integrations – enabling core components of the Composable Business Architecture – being able to sustain disruptive changes in the Business, Application and Technology domain, and continue "normal" operations (or more). This requires specific style of integrations – connecting Business Processes, Application Systems and Technology IT Components through decoupled patterns or connecting them lousily.
  5. Standard APIs first – we avoid building custom integrations as the Provider, but try to use standard APIs as much as possible. We try to “adjusting” for various Consumers by “compensating/complementing in Business APIs, or mostly in Experience APIs layer.
  6. …

These are only few examples, and there could be more. Each organization will set its own “strong” statements – but let’s not overdo – let’s keep it within range of 3-5 main principles, which are clear and implementable.

Then, Integration Patterns

While we may identify some specific patterns, specific for our organization, I will limit the list here on these five:

  • Sync Request-Reply
  • Async Request-Reply
  • PubSub
  • E(T)L
  • DB Data Replication

Sync Request-Reply pattern

Figure 1. Sync Request-Reply

When to use

  • Real-time needed
  • Low or medium volumes
  • (Lousily-)coupled

Rules:

  • Use API-M as an entry point
  • Use middleware (CPI) for mapping/orchestration

Anti-patterns:

  • Point-to-point integration from client to backend
  • Long-running sync calls – if so, use Async

Explained:

  • Sync Request-Reply is a single thread, so only one operation will run at a time. System sending Request will wait for that Request to be answered by the other System (=Response), before continuing with other operations. Usually, this is used to encapsulate the transaction(s).
  • Requestor (System sending Request Message) will wait for the response from the Responder (System sending Response Message).

Examples of use:

  • SAP Commerce Cloud and other Order taking channels in the Omni-channel landscape are creating/updating or reading Sales Orders via API-M[6] in SAP S/4HANA – where SAP S/4HANA is main Order fulfilment solution.
  • Omni-channel solutions are acquiring via API-M from SAP OPPs (SAP Omnichannel Promotion Pricing) relevant and available on-invoice Promotions.
  • …

Async Request-Reply pattern

Figure 2.-Async Request-Reply

When to use:

  • Near real time needed, but also no real-time for bulk payloads
  • Medium/high volumes
  • Response needed
  • Reliability and near-real time
  • Retry and DMQ/DLQ critical
  • Decoupled needed

Rules:

  • Use broker (AEM – Advanced Event Mesh) for choreography through Topics (incl. dynamic/smart) and Queues, and/or middleware (CPI) with queueing for mapping/orchestration
  • Guaranteed delivery
  • Idempotency is mandatory

Anti-patterns:

  • Building without queueing

Explained:

  • Async Request-Reply is a multi-thread, so System can run in parallel other operations – System sending Request Message will not wait for any Response, before continuing with other operations. The other System may still send back (at some point of time) separate Response Massage, e.g. to update the status of the processing etc. Although the exchange of Request Message and Response Message may be very fast in many cases (almost near-real time), there is no “guarantee”, no “time-out”, and System sending Request Message will not “block” its operations waiting for the Response Message.
  • Requestor (System sending Request Message) does not wait for the response, Responder (System sending Response Message) will send response "at some point of time".
  • No session freezing for the Requestor, Requester can work on other operations.

Examples of use:

  • SAP MDG is providing (Async) one-way synchronization of Material/Product data, BOM, Equipment etc. via AEM[7] Topics/Queues to various Subscribers – RM (Raw Materials) to MES (Manufacturing Execution System) solution, FG (Finish Goods) to Order taking channels – where each will provide confirmation.
  • SAP MDG is providing (Async) two-way synchronization of BP (Business Partner) Vendor/Supplier master data with Ariba – where the other system is sending back the confirmation after processing of the inbound records. This can include CPI[6], but can also be done (exceptionally) directly with DRF processing.
  • SAP S/4HANA is providing (Async) one-way synchronization of bulk delta Customer Hierarchies via CPI with DataStore (or JMS Queue) to Promo solution – where Promo solution is sending back the confirmation of the process batch.
  • …

PubSub pattern

Figure 3. PubSub

When to use:

  • Near real time needed, but also no real-time for bulk payloads
  • High volumes
  • Multiple Providers and Consumers
  • Decoupled needed

Rules:

  • Use broker (AEM – Advanced Event Mesh) for choreography through Topics (incl. dynamic/smart) and Queues
  • Use (optional) dedicated message broker replacing AEM Queues (i.e. Azure Service Bus provides native integration with Microsoft Dynamics 365).
  • Use (optional) middleware (CPI) for message adjustment (i.e. MI in AEM might not be sufficient).
  • Business events are distinct changes in a "state”.
  • Small payloads for Event Notifications, full records for Data Streming.

Anti-patterns:

  • Filtering or adjusting messages in the source for specific target systems – if needed, build smart and use the “power” of dynamic/smart Topics and MI in AEM.

Explained:

  • PubSub (Publish-Subscribe) is Async many-to-many communication between different Systems, decoupling the System that creates messages (the Publisher) from the System that process them (the Subscribers) by using a central intermediary known as a broker.
  • Publishers send messages to a specific Topic in the broker which ensures Subscriber(s) receive a copy of that message, making it highly effective for Event-Driven Architectures
  • Subscriber may be subscribed directly to the Topic or can be connected to a Queue which is subscribed to one or more Topics.

Examples of use:

  • SAP S/4HANA (i.e. via standard CloudEvents or custom add-on messages) and non-SAP “last-mile-delivery” solutions are publishing Event Notifications/Streaming to Omni-channel Order taking apps incl. web and mobile apps – thus providing multi-channel via ICaaS Notification Hub (emails, SMS, social etc.) notifications as well as in-app notifications, to Customers/Consumers and in-house users.
  • CRM solution is providing marketing campaign notifications via ICaaS Notification Hub (emails, SMS, social etc.).
  • SAP S/4HANA (i.e. via add-on “translating” legacy IDOC) is a publishing Data Streaming of Production Order transactional data to MES (Manufacturing Execution System) solutions.
  • …

E(T)L pattern

Figure 4. E(T)L

When to use:

  • No real-time needed
  • Large volume
  • Analytics / reporting use cases

Rules:

  • Use ETL tool or middleware (CPI) with queueing for pulling and pushing data on scheduled intervals
  • Prefer delta over full loads
  • Schedule outside business hours

Anti-pattern:

  • Using batch for operational processes

Explained:

  • E(T)L (Extract-Transform-Load) is a foundational data integration pattern to Extract large datasets from the source system and Load it into the target system.
  • Transformation is an optional process which may be happening in-between extraction and loading or can be happening in the source destination after loading of the datasets.

Examples of use:

  • Extract-and-Load of SAP MDG master data via CPI daily job to non-SAP planning solutions.
  • Extract-and-Load of SAP S/4HANA tables via Azure Data Factory to DWH solution (i.e. Azure DataMesh)
  • …

DB Data Replication pattern

Figure 5. DB Data Replication

When to use:

  • Real-time replication needed
  • Large volumes
  • Full table replication
  • Analytics / reporting use cases

Rules:

  • Use CDC tools (SLT – SAP Landscape Transformation) for instant data replication
  • Prefer delta over full replication

Anti-pattern:

  • Using for transactional processing

Explained:

  • This is DB (Database) table replication of records based on CDC (Change-Data-Capture) change-log.
  • While very powerful tool for “real” time replication, there are limitations – there is limited number od Consumers which can be served, can be intensive on DB server side, bounded with table structures, there are some limitations for tables without change logs or CDS views etc.

Examples of use:

  • Replicating SAP S/4HANA reference data tables via SLT[8] to related SAP MDG tables
  • Replicating SAP S/4HANA tables via SLT to non-SAP DWH solution
  • …

And do apply some “hard” rules…

Do not run-away for apply some rules…. Actually, do it in call cases! Of course, rules may differ from organization to organization, but here are some example:

  • No direct point-to-point integration
  • No bypassing API-M
  • No business logic
  • No backend exposing

Furthermore, thing about the Integration Governance… Setting up the Intgration Principles and defining Integration Pattens may be “easier” part of the overall Integration Guardrails – making it work, well this is usually more challenging…

  • All integrations have clearly defined owners from the respective product teams. This owner is responsible for ensuring the appropriate business context of any integration flow – remember, we do not connect “applications” we integrate “business processes”. Owner provides (business/functional) requests in sufficient (predefined) details, while respective Integration Architects will take over and develop solution (respecting Integration Principles and Appling appropriate Integration Patterns).
  • All developed APIs/EventAPIs must be clearly documented with versioning.
  • All teams must follow Integration Guidelines (i.e. on Performance Testing, Monitoring and Observability). This is cross-platform effort, especially in the Agile teams..
  • Design with backward (and planned forward) compatibility enforced
  • Integrations (no matter which tool is used) in its nature stateless (i.e. avoid extensive mapping, transformations, heavy orchestration rules). While these are old SOA principles, it should be still respected as mush as possible. Modern IPaaS Solutions (like CPI) or brokers (like Kafka with Flink SQL) can do a lot – but is the middleware the right place to keep the business logic? Do we want to hide business logic from the Providers (i.e. “inventing” new in-transit SalesOrder statuses other than those Sap S/4HANA provides)?
  • Security standards (i.e. OAuth).
  • …

The decision matrix – when to use what?

Things are (not so) simple? Well, let’s try to build some high-level decision matrix – when to apply which of the Integration Patterns (sorry no diagram, only pseudo-code):

If “Real time needed” then 
    If “Small/medium volumes” then 
        Sync Request-Reply
    Else 
        DB Data Replication
    End If;
Else If “Near real time needed” then
    If “Response needed” then 
        Async Request-Reply
    Else 
        PubSub
    End If;
Else if “No real time needed” then 
    If “Response needed” then 
        Async Request-Reply
    Else if “Multiple Providers and Consumers” then
        PubSub
    Else
        E(TL)
    End If;
End If;

Of course, this is just an example, and we can play with it…

Finally, building our SBBs?

While high-level Integration Patterns are great, this is just a start.

We need to build on top of the high-level Integration Patterns as they do not answer all the questions... As we know “devil is in the details” – depending which Provider(s) and Consumers(s) we are connecting, what Data Objects we are transferring, or what communication protocols we are using (HTTP or AMQP etc.) – “things” may be quite different.

Just a simple example for PubSub:

  • If we publish message from S/4HANA via AEM add-on what will be HTTP attributes we will use for monitoring/observability (i.e. assigning values to “Solace-Message-ID" and/or "Solace-Correlation-ID”) or for partitioning in case our recipient is not on HTTP (i.e. assigning values to "Solace-User-Property-JMSXGroupID”)?
  • Using and designing dynamic/smart topics, what are the requirements?
  • Persistent messages (in queue) or dynamic Topics to Subscriber (i.e. using other tools as a Message Queue like Azure Service Bus)?
  • For persistent design, what is retry and retention policy (time-to-live) and DMQ?
  • Retries and delayed retries in case recipient is not on HTTP?
  • What if the recipient is RDP (REST Delivery Point) on HTTP? We cannot use partitioning, but can we scale connections or consumers on RDP binding?
  • Also, if the recipient is RDP we cannot implement delay retries. What do we do? Is this a must (so we do this with CPI and JMS Queue on top), or can we skip it?
  • etc.

While it can get easier with "simple" Integration Patterns like Sync Request-Reply, it can also get more complex with "heavy” Integration Patterns like Async Request-Reply...

Leadership?

It's not only about how good artifacts (i.e. Integration Patterns or SBBs) we "build”, but it’s mostly about how we collaborate in bringing all this together Each organization will set its own rules – and it may differ significantly. Organizations may observe more or less agile approaches. Or organizations may have specific culture of work.

Still, here are some general areas to address when defining Roles and Responsibilities:

  • Who and how will refine Integration Patterns?
  • Who and how will refine existing or bring new SBBs?
  • Who and how will prepare new Integration requirements?
  • How do we ensure Integration Principles (i.e. REUSE) is observed?
  • How do we ensure Integration Patterns and SBBs are applied?
  • Who does functional design?
  • Who does technical design?
  • Who will do different testing phases (SIT, UAT, performance testing etc)
  • Who and how will implement monitoring and observability?
  • When and how do we pass the Integration Service to support?
  • What is the definition of ready?
  • What is the definition of done?
  • ...

At the end, putting all this together is about leadership – and no, I do not say it’s "one person" – it’s about how do we make "leadership happen" in all phases. Clearly this cannot be only EA or SA doing it all. We need to have all the "integration artifacts” make sense – it cannot be "disconnected” from the reality. Having delivery team members involved early enough is an absolute must.

Integration Guardrails are more than just Integration Patterns – it's more than just Integration Architecture as well. Guardrails are defining the “boundaries" we follow when “integrating everything with everyone”. Architecture is just one “piece” of the puzzle – but this puzzle has many pieces.  

Conclusion

The main purpose of this article is to:

  • Connect the “dots” from the past (previous articles and terminology) with new “simplified” view and (high-level) patterns.
  • Explain that we can leverage from the past experiences and continue with our SAP Integration Suite in future – using of course Advance Event Mesh much more, but not neglecting the usage of Cloud Integration and API-M, as it all “fits” our future needs.

Can we build decoupled and lousily-coupled integrations around ISA-M and it’s integration with SAP Integration Suite in (near) future? What will SAP Business Data Cloud bring as per “new” Integration Patterns around Data Products? And what are AI and MCP really bringing in the integration Patterns?

Many questions, but future is not “certain” always. Maybe in few years (or less) I will be refreshing the Integration Patterns article with some new “buzz-word”…

Acknowledgment

*) Intro photo Ales Krivec on Unsplash

References

[1] SAP Blog: Integration patterns for the Composable Business A... - SAP Community

[2] SAP Blog: What is Composable Architecture? - SAP Community

[3] Gartner: The Future of Business Is Composable - Gartner Keynote

[4] The OpenGroup: Boundaryless Information Flow™ | www.opengroup.org

[5] SAP ISA-M: Integration Methodology | Services and Support

[6] SAP CPI/API-M: SAP Integration Suite | Integration Software

[7] SAP AEM: Advanced Event Mesh | SAP Integration Suite

[8] SAP SLT: SAP Landscape Transformation Replication Server | SAP

entry.title: Re: Live Session: Get Certified - SAP Generative AI Developer | May 6–20, 2026
entry.content:
Exciting New Dates Are Now Available!

We’ve added several new series to help you find the perfect time to get started. Choose the schedule that works best—and join thousands of professionals gearing up to unlock their certification success:

Upcoming Live Session Dates

  • April 20 - April 22, 2026: Convenient 3-day session series with daily time slots to get you certified fast.
  • May 6 - May 20, 2026: Multiple time slots available to cater to your preferred schedule.
  • Aug 5 - Aug 19, 2026: Don’t miss our summer series, perfect for professionals and students prepping for future opportunities!

Why Get Certified?

Certification as a SAP Generative AI Developer ensures you stand out in a competitive market, as companies rapidly adopt AI-driven solutions to power their business transformation. With SAP’s cutting-edge practice systems and live sessions taught by top experts, you’ll gain real, applicable skills that will allow you to:

  • Confidently navigate the SAP Generative AI Hub and integrate AI into real enterprise solutions.
  • Master prompt engineering techniques to effectively address complex business scenarios.
  • Gain hands-on experience with the SAP Cloud SDK for AI, building advanced automation and solving real-world challenges.
  • Be exam-ready with expert instruction, live demonstrations, interactive Q&A conversations, and tailored preparation tips.

Who Should Attend?

This certification is perfect for:

  • Roles: Developers, Consultants, IT Experts, and AI enthusiasts looking to upskill in advanced technologies.
  • Experience Levels: Whether you’re a Beginner, Intermediate, or Advanced learner, you’ll gain the support you need to excel.

Exclusive Systems-Based Certification Practice

We’re committed to your success, and that’s why this series integrates innovative hands-on practice systems tailored to certification exam scenarios. These systems offer a live environment to apply your learnings directly, helping you practice for real-world applications and stay confident when tackling the exam.

Don’t Wait – Register Today!

The demand for SAP Generative AI-certified professionals is growing rapidly, and new dates fill up quickly. Secure your spot now to take your skills and career to the next level.

Find out more, explore the new dates, and register here!

entry.title: Re: What’s New, Now Relevant: Introducing Release Center
entry.content:
@aartidevikar Thank you for this article.  Some questions.  How does someone change the status on an assigned articled.  We enabled the Release Center, and view on the Release Center Object Permissions, but there is nothing that says Status:

"In the Release Center, you can assign articles to colleagues, set status on articles, and comment on articles. Use the collaborative features to keep track of your workflow. For example, you can assign an article to Mary for investigation, Mary can put it in progress, add comments to describe her findings, discuss it with you in a meeting, enable it, and then set it to a completed status."

Also, we noticed that when export the Release Center it contains the Enablement and Reference Number fields, but they don't show in the UI nor are they available to show via the "gears" or anywhere else.  

(Finally, we did a compare of the Release Center to the What's New Viewer of the "Titles".  Note that the Release Center showed 321 items for us, but the compare to What's New Viewer showed 306.  That means 15 items didn't return.  It appears that the Titles in the What's New View have spaces at the end.  So that is just a takeaway.

Let me know if I should open an SAP ticket on the few Items I've mentioned.

entry.title: Intercompany Asset Transfer
entry.content:
When transferring assets between Company codes using ABT1N. and selected No Revenue but still asking for Revenue GL's. don't know why?  Can we use IC clearing account between Assets as GL account to clear between 2 company codes.  the message is throwing like this.

Account 21800200 is defined as a balance sheet account in chart of accounts SSMH. However, this is not correct for circumstance Clearing acct.revenue from sale to affiliated comp.

You should note that the Customizing definition of the respective depreciation area also has an influence on the consistency check for the general ledger accounts. This particularly affects depreciation areas that are set up for managing investment support measures on the liabilities side. In order for the consistency check to be correct, you must set the correct indicator in the definition of the affected depreciation area.
